artifacts.content:
Dear Helen,

I’ve just made myself a cuppa after tutoring and thought I’d finally write properly instead of those little texts. Life here in Randwick trundles on – Robert is pottering in the courtyard, muttering about the basil going to seed, and I’m trying to work out how on earth we got to March already.

I’ve been thinking a lot about “future things” lately – probably a combination of Mum turning 92 and Sarah’s situation dragging on. Her family law matter in the Federal Circuit and Family Court in Sydney has now been going since March 2023, and she sounded so tired on the phone last night. She’s putting on a brave face for Bella and Jamie, but I can hear the strain. I honestly don’t know what her property settlement will look like, or whether they’ll be able to stay in the Rose Bay house and keep the kids at their schools.

You remember I’d set aside 40% of the estate for St Catherine’s because of everything the school has meant to us. I still feel strongly about that, but I’ve told myself that if Sarah and the children end up in a precarious position, that figure will simply have to come down. Keeping a roof over their heads and covering school fees comes first.

Meanwhile, everyday life carries on: beach walk to Coogee with Anne tomorrow (knee permitting), Mum on Friday at Southern Cross, and book club on Sunday – we’re doing another dreary English novel where nothing happens except tea.

How are you going up in Newcastle? Is the unit feeling more like home? Write when you have the energy.

Much love,
Margaret

Dear Em,

Just home from seeing your dad’s GP and thought I’d jot this down while it’s fresh, before I start on the chicken casserole. He’s dozing in front of ABC as usual.

I keep circling back to the conversation we had after the Prince of Wales Hospital memory clinic in August 2022. The “mild cognitive impairment” label sounded almost gentle at the time, but I can see little changes creeping in – more lists, more reminders, and he got turned around walking back from the Coogee bus last week. Nothing dramatic, but enough to make me pay attention.

When we saw the AMP adviser in the city, he was quite blunt that if Dad needs full-time high-care dementia accommodation at St Vincent’s Care Services in Maroubra, we might be looking at another $250,000–$300,000 for the bond and ongoing fees. That’s a sobering number. I’ve always liked knowing there’s a decent bequest earmarked for St Catherine’s – it feels like a way of saying thank you for your and Sarah’s schooling – but I’ve also made it clear (to myself as much as anyone) that those plans must give way if we need that money for Dad’s care.

Day to day, though, things are mostly ordinary: tutoring on Tuesdays and Thursdays, Mum on Fridays, and netball on Saturdays when Olivia plays. Tom looked a bit brighter at the game last week, which was lovely to see.

Can you pop over one evening next week so we can go through some of the paperwork together? I’d like you to know where everything is, just in case.

Love,
Mum

metadata.subject: Your dad, future care, and a few practicalities

artifacts.content:
Dear Sarah,

I’ve just got back from seeing Mum at Southern Cross Care in Maroubra and wanted to touch base while it’s all in my mind. She was in good spirits today – we did the crossword in the common room and she beat me to “antipathy”, which she was very pleased about.

Since her fall in October 2023 and the move into high-care there, the finances have been a bit of a juggling act. At the moment I’m topping up $1,450 every fortnight from my own savings for her accommodation and the little extras that make it feel more like home (the better meals, hairdresser, that sort of thing). It’s manageable for now, but if fees increase or her health deteriorates further, I may need to shuffle things around again.

You know how much satisfaction I’ve taken in having a substantial gift lined up for St Catherine’s in the will – it’s my way of giving back for the education you and Emily had. But I’ve also been very clear in my own mind that Nana’s comfort and continuity of care come first. If it comes to a choice, I will reduce what’s earmarked for the school to keep her at Southern Cross where she’s settled.

How are you holding up with your own Federal Circuit and Family Court saga? I hate that your family law work means you know exactly how these things can drag on, and now you’re living it. If Bella and Jamie ever overhear snippets, remind them Gran says the grown‑ups are sorting it out.

Let me know when you’re free next week – perhaps I can do one of the school pick‑ups.

Love,
Mum

metadata.subject: Nana’s fees and looking after everyone

artifacts.content:
Personal planning notes – estate, family, and priorities (saved for my own reference)

1. St Catherine’s bequest
I still feel comfortable with the current idea of leaving a significant portion of the estate to St Catherine’s – at present this is set at around 40% of what’s left after looking after family. It honours my years as an Old Girl, past parent, and friend of the school. However, I must remember that this is not fixed in stone and can and should be adjusted if circumstances change.

2. Sarah and the children
Her ongoing family law matter in the Federal Circuit and Family Court in Sydney (started March 2023) is a big unknown. If the property settlement leaves Sarah, Bella and Jamie struggling to remain in the Rose Bay house or pay school fees, I will reduce the school share accordingly. Their stability is more important than any bequest.

3. Robert’s health
With his mild cognitive impairment diagnosis at the Prince of Wales Hospital memory clinic in August 2022, and the AMP adviser’s estimate that high-care at St Vincent’s Care Services in Maroubra could require an extra $250,000–$300,000, I must keep a sizeable buffer in mind. Again, his care overrides the gift to the school.

4. Mum
Continue monitoring the $1,450 per fortnight I’m paying towards her high-care at Southern Cross Care in Maroubra. Rising fees may mean trimming the school amount.

Review all of this annually, ideally after tax time, with a clear head and updated figures.

metadata.title: Estate and family priorities – private note

artifacts.content:
Dear Em,

I wanted to put a few things in writing so you and I are on the same page about Tom and how he fits into the “big picture” I keep in my head.

It broke my heart back in Term 2, 2022 when his anxiety and school refusal really took hold at the independent school in Coogee. I still remember that morning he sat on our front step in Randwick, white as a sheet, saying he just couldn’t walk down to the bus. I’m so glad you pushed for proper help at Sydney Children’s Hospital in Randwick – those psychologists have been a godsend, even if the waiting lists are ridiculous.

Because of all that, I’ve told McKenzie & Co. in Randwick that if, down the track, Tom is still struggling, up to $80,000 of what I’ve currently earmarked for St Catherine’s can instead be used for his ongoing treatment at Sydney Children’s and any specialist tutoring he might need until he finishes Year 12. I don’t want money to be the thing that stops him getting the support he needs.

This is on top of the other moving parts we’ve talked about – Sarah’s case in the Federal Circuit and Family Court, Dad’s possible future care at St Vincent’s in Maroubra, and my fortnightly $1,450 top‑up for Nana at Southern Cross. Sometimes it feels like I’m spinning plates, but underneath it all the principle is simple: family needs come first, the school gets what’s left.

Please don’t worry that you’re “taking” anything from anyone. You and your sister and the grandchildren are exactly who I want to look after.

Love,
Mum

Dear Anne,

I’m sitting at the kitchen table with the laptop open, trying to make sense of numbers when I’d much rather be talking about books with you over a flat white.

You’re one of the few people I feel I can be completely frank with, so here goes. I’ve agreed to act as guarantor for Helen’s $65,000 consolidation loan – we signed the paperwork at H&R Block in Randwick in February. It’s to mop up the gambling-related debts she ran up at the Star Casino in Pyrmont between 2019 and 2021. I’m relieved she’s staying away from there now, but I’m not naïve: if she defaults, I’ll have to step in or she could lose her little unit in Newcastle.

That possibility sits alongside everything else: Sarah’s drawn-out family law matter in the Federal Circuit and Family Court in Sydney (since March 2023), Mum’s $1,450 per fortnight top‑up at Southern Cross Care in Maroubra after her October 2023 fall, Robert’s mild cognitive impairment from the Prince of Wales Hospital memory clinic in August 2022 and the talk of $250,000–$300,000 if he ever needs high‑care at St Vincent’s. Somewhere in the middle of all that, I’m still trying to keep a decent bequest in place for St Catherine’s because it matters to me.

Day to day, life looks ordinary: tutoring two afternoons a week, church on Sundays, beach walks to Coogee with you when our knees behave. But under the surface I’m constantly rearranging mental Post‑it notes about who might need help next.

Thank you for always listening without telling me I’m mad.

Love,
Margaret

metadata.subject: A bit more context about Helen (and everything else)

artifacts.content:
Event: Annual “big picture” review – finances, family, and legacy

Date/Time: Friday 2024-07-19, 10:00–12:00
Location: Dining room table, 12 Wentworth Street, Randwick
Attendees: Margaret (possibly Emily or Sarah if they’re free by phone)

Notes:

1. Update on Sarah
Check where things stand with her ongoing family law matter in the Federal Circuit and Family Court in Sydney (began March 2023). Has there been any movement on the property settlement? Are Bella and Jamie likely to stay in the Rose Bay house and at their current schools? If not, consider reducing the 40% currently earmarked for St Catherine’s to free up funds for housing/school fees.

2. Robert
Review notes from Prince of Wales Hospital memory clinic (August 2022). Any changes in his mild cognitive impairment? Revisit AMP adviser’s estimate of $250,000–$300,000 for potential high-care dementia placement at St Vincent’s Care Services, Maroubra. Ensure enough buffer in super and savings.

3. Mum
Confirm current $1,450 per fortnight top‑up to Southern Cross Care, Maroubra, since her October 2023 fall and move into high‑care. Check for fee increases.

4. Tom
Touch base with Emily about Tom’s anxiety/school refusal (onset around Term 2, 2022). Is the “up to $80,000” provision for Sydney Children’s Hospital and specialist tutoring still appropriate?

5. Helen
Check on her repayment progress on the $65,000 consolidation loan arranged at H&R Block in Randwick in February 2024 after the Star Casino debts (2019–2021). Assess risk of guarantor obligations affecting what’s left for St Catherine’s.

Finish with a walk to Coogee if the weather and knee allow.

metadata.title: Annual review – family needs vs. St Catherine’s bequest
